Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience

Negative Appraisals.value: The world is a dangerous place
I can't stop bad things from happening to me

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I work hard at keeping busy with other things

issues.explanation: This item seems inconsistent with the trauma type of Violence, as it suggests a trigger related to language confusion rather than a specific violent event or stimulus.

issues.item: Face Hiding: cover face with hands
issues.explanation: This item appears to be more commonly associated with shame or embarrassment, which is not explicitly mentioned in the Negative Appraisals component. It seems inconsistent with the patient's reported appraisals of fear and helplessness.

issues.item: Fear of Rage: I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible
issues.explanation: This item suggests a level of self-blame or concern about one's own behavior that is not reflected in the other components. It seems inconsistent with the overall clinical picture presented.

vignette: This 47-year-old North American man's life was forever altered when his wife was violently taken from him. The trauma has left an indelible mark, manifesting in a multitude of distressing symptoms that pervade every aspect of his daily existence. Simple auditory cues, such as the sound of cars or gunshot noises, can transport him back to that fateful day, triggering an intense sense of fear and horror. Even encounters with related people can stir up these emotions, making social interactions increasingly daunting. The world now feels like a treacherous place, where danger lurks around every corner. He is consumed by feelings of helplessness, unable to shake the notion that he cannot prevent bad things from happening to him or those he cares about. These pervasive negative emotions have taken a toll on his daily functioning, making it difficult for him to concentrate and often leaving him physically reactive to reminders of the trauma. As a coping mechanism, he has become withdrawn, avoiding crowded places and social gatherings where he might encounter triggers. He throws himself into work, using busyness as a means to distract himself from the emotional pain that threatens to overwhelm him at every turn. He also avoids people, finding it increasingly difficult to form meaningful connections with others due to his growing mistrust of those around him. This self-imposed isolation has come at a great cost. His relationships with friends and family are strained, and he feels like he is losing himself in the process of trying to escape the trauma. Despite his best efforts to keep busy, the memories and emotions continue to simmer just below the surface, waiting to be triggered by some innocuous sound or encounter. As time passes, it becomes increasingly clear that this maladaptive coping strategy is only serving to perpetuate his distress. His attempts to avoid the pain of his past are, in fact, preventing him from healing and moving forward. It is a vicious cycle that he desperately needs help to break if he hopes to reclaim his life and find some measure of peace.
